question_text: Raman Textiles Ltd, a seasonal business, experiences peak sales during Diwali season. During off-season, its sales drop by 60%. Which working capital approach is most suitable?
explanation: Seasonal businesses have fluctuating working capital needs. During peak season (Diwali), they need significantly more working capital for inventory and production. During off-season, demand drops. The company should maintain a permanent base of working capital and arrange temporary/variable working capital for the peak season.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Maintain maximum working capital throughout the year
Maintain base working capital permanently and arrange additional funds for peak season
Maintain no working capital during off-season
Convert all working capital into fixed capital during off-season

elimination_hints.hint: Maintaining peak-level working capital year-round means idle funds during off-season — wasteful.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising the cost of idle funds

elimination_hints.hint: Even in off-season, some operations continue — minimum working capital is always needed.
Working capital and fixed capital serve different purposes — converting between them is impractical.

question_text: Priya Foods Ltd produces perishable goods (dairy products) while Raj Steel Ltd produces durable goods. Which company will need more working capital and why?
explanation: Priya Foods (perishable goods) needs more working capital because perishable goods cannot be stored for long, requiring frequent and rapid production cycles. This means faster turnover of raw materials, higher inventory management needs, and quick conversion into cash. The operating cycle is shorter but more intensive.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Priya Foods — perishable goods need rapid and frequent production cycles
Raj Steel — durable goods need more raw material storage
Both need the same working capital regardless of product type
Neither needs working capital as both are manufacturing companies

elimination_hints.hint: Durable goods can be stored and sold gradually — they have a slower, more manageable cycle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking durability means more storage cost

elimination_hints.hint: Product nature directly impacts inventory turnover and operating cycle, affecting working capital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring product nature's effect on working capital

elimination_hints.hint: All manufacturing companies need working capital for raw materials, production, and sales.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking manufacturing needs no working capital

question_text: Meera starts a new IT services company. She has two options: (i) 100% equity of Rs 2 crore, or (ii) Rs 1 crore equity + Rs 1 crore debt at 12% interest. Expected EBIT is Rs 40 lakh and tax rate is 25%. Which statement is correct?
explanation: Option (i): EBT = 40 lakh, Tax = 10 lakh, PAT = 30 lakh, ROE = 30/200 = 15%. Option (ii): Interest = 12 lakh, EBT = 28 lakh, Tax = 7 lakh, PAT = 21 lakh, ROE = 21/100 = 21%. The debt option gives higher ROE (21% vs 15%) because ROI (20%) > cost of debt (12%).
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Option (i) gives higher ROE of 20%
Both options give the same ROE of 15%
Option (ii) gives higher ROE of 21% due to leverage
Option (ii) gives lower ROE because interest reduces profit

elimination_hints.hint: With 100% equity: PAT = 30 lakh, ROE = 30/200 = 15%, not 20%.
The capital structure difference creates different ROEs — leverage boosts equity returns.
While interest reduces total profit, the ROE is calculated on a SMALLER equity base (Rs 1 cr vs 2 cr), giving higher percentage.
elimination_hints.misconception: Focusing only on absolute profit, not percentage return

question_text:
Consider the following statements about financial planning:
(I) It reduces uncertainty by anticipating future financial needs.
(II) It helps in coordinating various business functions.
(III) It ensures 100% accuracy in financial forecasts.
(IV) It helps in avoiding business shocks and surprises.

Which statements are correct?
explanation: Statements I, II, and IV are correct. Financial planning reduces (not eliminates) uncertainty (I), coordinates production, sales, and other functions through fund allocation (II), and helps avoid shocks (IV). Statement III is incorrect — no planning can ensure 100% accuracy as the future is inherently uncertain.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: I, II, III, and IV
I, II, and IV only
I and IV only
III and IV only

elimination_hints.hint: Statement III claims '100% accuracy' — no planning can guarantee this. Future is uncertain.
Statement II is also correct — financial planning coordinates various business functions through fund allocation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Missing the coordination benefit of financial planning

elimination_hints.hint: Statement III is wrong (100% accuracy impossible), and Statements I and II are correct too.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting 100% accuracy while missing other correct statements

question_text: Global Pharma Ltd has high fixed costs due to R&D expenses. During an economic downturn, its sales dropped by 30%. Which financial challenge will the company face?
explanation: High fixed costs (R&D, machinery) mean the company must earn enough revenue to cover these costs regardless of sales volume. A 30% sales drop while fixed costs remain the same creates a cash flow crisis — the company may struggle to pay fixed obligations like interest and fixed expenses.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Its fixed capital requirement will decrease proportionally
Its shareholders will demand more dividends
Its working capital needs will increase dramatically
Cash flow pressure due to inability to reduce fixed costs despite lower revenue

elimination_hints.hint: Fixed capital (already invested in R&D and equipment) doesn't decrease just because sales drop.
With declining sales, there's less profit — shareholders would expect lower dividends, not more.
With lower sales, working capital typically decreases (less inventory, fewer receivables).
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking lower sales increase working capital

question_text:
A finance manager must decide between two projects:
Project A: Cost Rs 50 lakh, Expected return 18%, Risk — Low
Project B: Cost Rs 50 lakh, Expected return 25%, Risk — High

Which factor should be the PRIMARY consideration?
explanation: The finance manager should consider the risk-return trade-off. Project B offers higher returns but with higher risk. The decision depends on the company's risk appetite and financial position. A prudent approach balances risk and return rather than choosing purely on return or purely on risk.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Always choose the highest return regardless of risk
Always choose the lowest risk regardless of return
Evaluate the risk-return trade-off based on company's risk tolerance
Choose neither and invest in fixed deposits only

elimination_hints.hint: Ignoring risk can lead to catastrophic losses — return alone is not sufficient.
Always choosing lowest risk means missing growth opportunities — too conservative.
elimination_hints.misconception: Risk-averse to the point of missing opportunities

elimination_hints.hint: Fixed deposits offer very low returns — a business should invest in its operations for growth.

question_text: A company has the following data: Debt = Rs 40 lakh at 10% interest, Equity = Rs 60 lakh, EBIT = Rs 20 lakh, Tax = 30%. What is the EPS if there are 60,000 equity shares?
explanation: Interest = 10% × 40 lakh = Rs 4 lakh. EBT = 20 − 4 = Rs 16 lakh. Tax = 30% × 16 = Rs 4.8 lakh. PAT = 16 − 4.8 = Rs 11.2 lakh. EPS = 11,20,000 / 60,000 = Rs 18.67 per share.
subject: cuet-business-studies

elimination_hints.hint: Rs 33.33 = 20 lakh / 60,000. You forgot to deduct interest AND tax.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using EBIT instead of PAT for EPS

elimination_hints.hint: Rs 26.67 = 16 lakh / 60,000. You deducted interest but forgot to deduct tax.
Rs 16 = 16 lakh / 1 lakh. Check the number of shares (60,000 not 1,00,000).

question_text: Which of the following is a consequence of inadequate working capital?
explanation: Inadequate working capital means the company doesn't have enough funds for day-to-day operations. This leads to inability to pay suppliers on time, loss of cash discounts, difficulty in meeting obligations, lower creditworthiness, and ultimately operational disruptions.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Loss of reputation and inability to pay creditors on time
Excessive idle funds leading to low returns
Over-investment in inventory causing wastage
Unnecessary speculation and risky investments

elimination_hints.hint: Excessive idle funds is a problem of SURPLUS working capital, not inadequate.
Over-investment in inventory is a surplus working capital problem.
Speculation with excess funds is a surplus working capital issue.

question_text: Which of the following is a consequence of EXCESS working capital?
explanation: Excess working capital means funds are lying idle without productive use. This leads to unnecessary accumulation of inventory (risk of wastage, theft, obsolescence), speculative investments, and lower overall return on investment. Idle funds earn no return.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Inability to pay suppliers on time
Loss of goodwill among creditors
Disruption in production due to lack of materials
Idle funds, unnecessary inventory accumulation, and lower returns

elimination_hints.hint: Inability to pay suppliers is caused by INADEQUATE working capital, not excess.
Loss of goodwill results from delayed payments — a problem of shortage, not excess.
Production disruption from material shortage is an inadequate working capital problem.

question_text: Star Electronics Ltd wants to expand its operations. The management decides to retain 80% of profits and distribute only 20% as dividends. Which factor is primarily influencing this dividend decision?
explanation: The company's growth and expansion plans are the primary factor. When a company has profitable investment opportunities, it retains more earnings to finance expansion internally rather than distributing them as dividends. This reduces dependence on external financing.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Legal restrictions on dividend payment
Shareholders' preference for current income
Growth and expansion opportunities of the company
Tax implications on shareholders

elimination_hints.hint: Legal restrictions set the maximum, not the company's choice to retain 80%.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing legal limits with strategic choice

elimination_hints.hint: If shareholders wanted current income, the company would distribute MORE, not less.
While tax matters, the scenario clearly states expansion as the reason for retention.

question_text:
Consider the following scenarios:
(I) Company A has stable earnings and no growth plans — pays 90% dividend.
(II) Company B is a startup with high growth plans — retains 95% profits.
(III) Company C faces legal restrictions — limits dividend to 10%.
(IV) Company D has contractual obligation with lenders restricting dividends.

Which scenarios correctly reflect factors affecting dividend decision?
explanation: All four scenarios correctly represent different factors: (I) Stability of earnings and growth opportunities, (II) Growth prospects requiring retention, (III) Legal constraints (e.g., Companies Act provisions), (IV) Contractual obligations (loan covenants). All are valid factors affecting dividend decisions.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: I and II only
I, II, and III only
II and IV only
I, II, III, and IV — all are correct

elimination_hints.hint: III and IV also represent valid dividend factors — legal and contractual constraints.
IV (contractual obligation) is also a valid factor — lenders often restrict dividends.
I and III are also correct — stable earnings and legal restrictions affect dividends.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring earnings stability and legal factors

question_text: A company's control consideration in financing decision means:
explanation: Control consideration refers to the existing shareholders' desire to maintain control over the company. Issuing new equity shares dilutes ownership, while raising debt doesn't dilute control. Therefore, companies wanting to retain control prefer debt over equity.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Existing owners prefer debt to avoid dilution of their ownership stake
The company wants to control the interest rate on debt
The government controls the company's financing decisions
The company wants to control the stock market price

elimination_hints.hint: Control consideration is about ownership control, not controlling interest rates.
Government doesn't usually control private financing decisions — control means ownership control.
Controlling stock price is market management, not the 'control consideration' in financing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing stock price management with ownership control

question_text: Flexibility in capital structure means:
explanation: Flexibility refers to the ability to adjust the capital structure as per changing conditions. A company should be able to raise additional funds when needed and repay when not required. Excessive reliance on any single source reduces flexibility.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: The company can change its products anytime
The ability to raise or repay funds as per changing business needs
The company can change its location frequently
The company can hire and fire employees at will

elimination_hints.hint: Product flexibility is operational, not financial capital structure flexibility.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing product flexibility with financial flexibility

elimination_hints.hint: Location changes are operational decisions, not capital structure flexibility.
Employment flexibility is an HR matter, not capital structure.

question_text: During inflation, a company's working capital requirement will generally:
explanation: During inflation, the prices of raw materials, labour, and other inputs increase. The company needs more funds to purchase the same quantity of materials and maintain operations. Therefore, working capital requirement increases during inflationary periods.
subtopic: Price level changes and working capital
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Increase, as higher prices require more funds for the same operations
Decrease, as inflation reduces the real value of debt
Remain unchanged, as inflation affects only fixed capital
Decrease, as customers pay higher prices

elimination_hints.hint: While inflation reduces real debt value, the company still needs more nominal funds for operations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Focusing on debt benefit while ignoring operational cost increase

elimination_hints.hint: Inflation increases the cost of raw materials, wages etc. — all working capital items.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking inflation only affects fixed assets

elimination_hints.hint: Even if selling prices rise, input costs also rise — and often input costs rise first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming revenue increase offsets cost increase immediately

question_text: A company should consider stock market conditions when making a financing decision because:
explanation: During a bullish (rising) market, investors are optimistic and willing to invest in equity shares. This makes it easier and cheaper to raise equity capital. During a bearish market, equity issuance is difficult and the company should prefer debt. Stock market conditions directly affect the ease and cost of equity issuance.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: The company's fixed capital requirement changes with market conditions
The company can predict future stock prices with certainty
Debt becomes unavailable during a bearish market
Bullish markets make equity issuance easier and cheaper

elimination_hints.hint: Fixed capital depends on business needs, not stock market conditions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing fixed capital with market conditions

elimination_hints.hint: No one can predict stock prices with certainty — markets are inherently uncertain.
Debt availability depends on creditworthiness and interest rates, not directly on stock market trends.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing equity market with debt availability

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a factor affecting working capital?
explanation: Factors affecting working capital include: nature of business, scale of operations, business cycle, seasonal factors, production cycle, credit allowed, credit availed, operating efficiency, availability of raw materials, and price level changes. Goodwill of the promoter is not a direct factor affecting working capital requirements.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Scale of operations
Production cycle length
Credit allowed to customers
Goodwill of the promoter in social media

elimination_hints.hint: Larger scale means more raw materials, inventory, etc. — directly affects working capital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking scale doesn't affect working capital

elimination_hints.hint: Longer production cycle means more funds blocked in WIP — increases working capital.
More credit to customers increases receivables — directly raises working capital needs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking credit policy doesn't affect working capital

question_text: Which of the following best describes the role of a finance manager?
explanation: A finance manager's role encompasses estimating fund requirements, determining capital structure, selecting sources of funds, investing surplus funds, and ensuring adequate returns. The primary role is to manage the financial resources to maximise shareholder wealth.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Only keeping records of all financial transactions
Estimating fund requirements, deciding capital structure, and ensuring optimal use of funds
Only preparing annual budgets for the board
Only managing relationships with banks

elimination_hints.hint: Record-keeping is accounting. Finance manager's role is much broader — planning and decision-making.
Budget preparation is just one small part of a finance manager's broader role.
Bank relationships are one aspect — the role includes all financing, investing, and dividend decisions.

question_text: Identify the correct sequence of the operating cycle of a manufacturing company:
explanation: The operating cycle of a manufacturing company follows: Cash → Raw Materials (purchase) → Work-in-Progress (production) → Finished Goods (completion) → Debtors/Receivables (sale on credit) → Cash (collection). This cycle represents the time from cash outflow to cash inflow.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Cash → Raw Materials → WIP → Finished Goods → Debtors → Cash
Cash → Finished Goods → WIP → Raw Materials → Debtors → Cash
Debtors → Cash → Raw Materials → WIP → Finished Goods
Cash → Debtors → Finished Goods → WIP → Raw Materials → Cash

elimination_hints.hint: Production starts with raw materials, goes through WIP, then finished goods — not the reverse.
The cycle starts with cash outflow (buying raw materials), not with debtors.
Debtors come AFTER selling finished goods, not immediately after cash.

question_text: A company's Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR) helps in determining:
explanation: DSCR measures a company's ability to service its debt — i.e., pay interest and repay principal. A higher DSCR indicates better ability to meet debt obligations. This ratio is crucial for lenders when deciding whether to extend credit.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: The amount of dividend to be paid to shareholders
The working capital requirement of the company
The company's ability to pay interest and repay debt obligations
The company's market share in the industry

elimination_hints.hint: DSCR relates to debt servicing, not dividend decisions.
Working capital is calculated from current assets/liabilities, not DSCR.
Market share is a competitive/marketing metric, not a debt servicing measure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing financial ratios with market metrics

question_text:
Which of the following statements about financial management is CORRECT?
(I) Investment decisions are reversible and can be changed easily.
(II) Financing decisions determine the capital structure of the firm.
(III) Dividend decisions affect the amount of retained earnings available.
(IV) Financial planning should be rigid and unchangeable.
explanation: Statements II and III are correct. Financing decisions do determine the debt-equity mix (capital structure) (II). Dividend decisions directly affect retained earnings — higher dividends mean less retained earnings (III). Statement I is wrong — investment decisions (capital budgeting) are generally irreversible. Statement IV is wrong — financial planning should be flexible.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: I and IV only
II and III only
I, II, and III only
All four are correct

elimination_hints.hint: Both I and IV are incorrect — investment decisions are largely irreversible and financial planning should be flexible.
Statement I is wrong — capital budgeting decisions involve large, irreversible commitments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking investment decisions can be easily reversed

elimination_hints.hint: Statements I and IV are clearly incorrect — not all four can be correct.
